intro:
Galilee (meaning: wheel; revolution, or simply district) was the northernmost region of ancient Palestine, bounded by Lebanon to the north, the Jordan and Sea of Galilee to the east, the Jezreel Valley to the south, and the Mediterranean coastal plain to the west. Isaiah called it Galilee of the nations (Isaiah 9:1; cited in Matthew 4:15), reflecting its mixed ethnic composition and proximity to Gentile territories. The region encompassed fertile valleys, forested hills, and the prosperous fishing communities of the Sea of Galilee.

Galilee was the primary theater of Jesus's earthly ministry. He grew up in Nazareth, called his first disciples from among Galilean fishermen, performed the majority of his miracles, and delivered most of his recorded teaching in the towns and villages of this region. The early church in Galilee was established following the Resurrection (Acts 9:31). Despite its peripheral status in the eyes of Jerusalem's religious establishment, Galilee became the cradle of the Christian movement, and it is to Galilee that the risen Christ directed his disciples to meet him (Matthew 28:10).
